content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Before AI coding tools, I knew exactly what “being a software engineer” meant for me.
I wrote code, debugged it, iterated on designs, and slowly built up a sense of craft and confidence. The feedback loop was clear: I thought about a problem, implemented a solution, and learned from what worked (and what didn’t).
Then tools like Claude Code showed up.
Suddenly, a lot of the work that used to prove to me that I was “good at my job” started happening somewhere else — inside an AI assistant. I still design systems, review code, and make decisions, but the actual act of typing out solutions is increasingly automated.
And that’s created a strange internal shift:
I’m not always sure what I am contributing anymore, or how to think about my role.
The Quiet Identity Crisis Behind AI-Assisted Coding
We talk a lot about productivity gains, but not enough about the identity impact.
When AI handles the bulk of the implementation, it can trigger questions like:

• Am I still a “real” engineer if AI writes most of my code?

• If I’m not the one producing the solution line by line, what exactly is my craft?

• Would I still know how to solve this problem without the assistant?
For many of us, this is essentially an AI-assisted identity crisis.
It’s not that we’ve lost our skills overnight — it’s that the visible proof of those skills has moved from our hands to a tool. The work we used to equate with our value (writing code) is shifting to a different layer (thinking, reviewing, orchestrating), and our self-image hasn’t fully caught up.
Dependence vs. Delegation
There’s a subtle but important distinction between depending on AI and delegating to it.

• Delegation is intentional: “I understand the problem and the solution space; I’m using AI as an accelerator.”

• Dependence is quieter: “I’m not sure I could solve this without AI, and that uncertainty is starting to bother me.”
Many engineers — including me — have had moments where we realize we’re asking AI to think before we give ourselves a chance to think. Over time, that can erode confidence, even if the output is technically correct.
The uncomfortable truth: you can feel more productive while feeling less capable.
This Feels a Lot Like Impostor Syndrome (But With AI)
Classic impostor syndrome is about discounting your achievements and fearing you’ll be “found out.” AI just gives it a new flavor:

• “The system designs are mine, but the code is not, so maybe the hard part isn’t mine either.”

• “If AI disappeared tomorrow, would I still be able to perform at this level?”

• “Are people overestimating me because they don’t see how much AI I use?”
The paradox: our responsibilities are evolving upward — toward architecture, problem framing, and decision-making — yet we often still measure our worth in lines of code.
Redefining What It Means to “Do the Work”
If AI is here to stay (and it is), we need a healthier definition of engineering work.
Here’s a version I’ve found more helpful:

• The core of software engineering is understanding problems, designing reliable systems, and making good trade-offs.

• Code is the expression of those decisions — not the whole identity of the engineer.

• AI can accelerate expression, but it cannot replace understanding or accountability.
From this perspective, using tools like Claude Code doesn’t diminish our role; it just shifts the center of gravity. Our value moves more clearly into:

• How we frame the problem

• How we evaluate options

• How we reason about risks, edge cases, and long-term maintenance

• How we integrate the AI’s suggestions into coherent, robust systems
The work changes — but it doesn’t disappear.
Practical Ways I’m Reclaiming My Sense of Agency
To reduce that feeling of “I’m not really thinking about the solution,” I’ve started adopting some simple habits:

• Think first, prompt second
I sketch the solution in plain language or pseudocode before touching AI. If I can’t articulate an approach, that’s my cue to slow down and think.

• Use AI as a collaborator, not an oracle
I ask for alternatives: “Give me three different approaches and their trade-offs.” Then I choose and edit, instead of copy-pasting the first answer.

• Review AI-generated code like it came from a junior engineer
I treat the output as a draft. I question every assumption, check for edge cases, and refactor where needed. The mental model is still mine.

• Regularly solve small problems without AI
I intentionally pick small scripting tasks or algorithmic exercises and do them solo to keep my raw problem-solving muscles active.

• Measure my value in outcomes, not keystrokes
I track impact (features shipped, reliability improved, complexity reduced) rather than how many lines of code I personally wrote.
These habits don’t eliminate AI dependency overnight, but they turn it into structured delegation instead of unconscious reliance.
Why This Matters for Our Careers
AI will keep getting better at writing code. That’s a feature, not a bug.
The engineers who thrive won’t be the ones who avoid AI; they’ll be the ones who:

• Maintain strong problem-solving instincts

• Understand systems deeply enough to supervise AI effectively

• Can explain, defend, and adapt solutions over time

• Are comfortable saying, “I am accountable for this, even if AI helped implement it”
Our careers are less about being the fastest typists and more about being the best thinkers, communicators, and decision-makers in technical contexts.
The tools change; the need for human judgment does not.
A Question for Other Engineers
If you’re a developer using tools like Claude Code, Copilot, or Cursor:

• How has AI changed the way you think about your identity as an engineer?

• Do you feel more empowered, more dependent, or some mix of both?

• What habits help you stay confident that you still “know what you’re doing”?
I’d love to hear how others are navigating this new phase of our profession.
